content:
How to use Ornith 1.0 for free?

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Element5 Digital on UnsplashOpen-source coding models have improved dramatically over the past year. However, most reinforcement learning based coding models still rely on manually designed workflows, predefined agent scaffolds, and carefully engineered execution pipelines. These handcrafted systems determine how an AI reasons, writes code, executes tests, fixes bugs, and retries failed solutions.
Ornith-1.0, released by DeepReinforce.AI in June 2026, takes a completely different approach.
Instead of only learning how to write code, Ornith also learns how to build its own coding workflow.
The model continuously improves not only its code generation abilities but also the orchestration strategy used to solve software engineering problems. This idea is known as Self-Scaffolding, and it represents one of the most interesting research directions in agentic coding.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Built on top of pretrained Gemma 4 and Qwen 3.5 models, Ornith spans multiple model sizes, from lightweight edge-deployable 9B models to a massive 397B Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model. Despite being fully open source, Ornith achieves performance comparable to or better than several frontier proprietary coding models on demanding software engineering benchmarks.
In this article, we’ll explore how Ornith works, why self-scaffolding matters, its reinforcement learning pipeline, benchmark results, and why this architecture could influence the next generation of autonomous coding agents.

What is Ornith-1.0?
Ornith-1.0 is a family of reinforcement-learning-based Large Language Models specifically optimized for agentic coding.
Unlike conventional code generation models that simply predict the next token, Ornith is trained to solve complete software engineering tasks involving:

• Multi-step reasoning

• Repository understanding

• Terminal execution

• Tool usage

• Debugging

• Iterative code repair

• Software engineering workflows
The model family includes four variants targeting different deployment environments.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The larger models target enterprise-grade autonomous software engineering, while the compact 9B model demonstrates that surprisingly capable coding agents can also run efficiently on smaller hardware.

The Biggest Innovation: Self-Scaffolding
Traditional RL training for coding models follows a fairly rigid pipeline. A human engineer designs a workflow that looks something like this:
Task → Generate Code → Execute Tests → Retry → Return Answer
Every coding task follows essentially the same scaffold.This has worked reasonably well, but it introduces an important limitation.
The AI is only learning how to solve problems, not how to improve the problem-solving process itself.
Ornith changes this entirely. Instead of treating the scaffold as fixed, Ornith treats it as another object that can be optimized during reinforcement learning.
This means the model learns:

• how many reasoning steps to perform

• when to call tools

• how to structure memory

• when to retry

• how to debug

• how to organize execution
The workflow itself becomes trainable. This is what DeepReinforce calls Self-Scaffolding.

How Self-Scaffolding Works
Each reinforcement learning iteration consists of two stages.
Stage 1: Generate the Scaffold
Rather than immediately solving the coding task, the model first creates an execution strategy. That scaffold may define:

• reasoning sequence

• tool invocation order

• memory organization

• debugging strategy

• retry policy

• execution planning
Instead of humans writing these instructions, the model writes them itself.
Stage 2: Solve the Coding Task
Using the generated scaffold, Ornith then performs the actual coding task. This may involve:

• reading repository files

• editing code

• executing terminal commands

• running tests

• fixing compilation errors

• generating patches
After execution, the reward is propagated back to both the generated solution and the scaffold that produced it. As a result, successful workflows survive while ineffective workflows gradually disappear.
Over thousands of RL iterations, the scaffolds themselves evolve.
The model effectively discovers increasingly efficient ways to solve software engineering tasks without requiring researchers to manually engineer new agent pipelines.

Why This Matters
Nearly every modern coding agent, whether based on OpenHands, Claude Code, Cursor, or other frameworks depends on carefully hand-crafted orchestration logic. Developers spend considerable effort designing:

• retry mechanisms

• memory management

• planning systems

• tool ordering

• execution pipelines
Ornith attempts to automate this engineering process. Instead of humans continuously improving agent workflows, reinforcement learning discovers better workflows automatically.
This shifts optimization from handcrafted engineering to learned behavior, allowing the model to adapt its reasoning strategy to different categories of software engineering tasks.
